name: 1421_B. Putting Bricks in the Wall
description:
Pink Floyd are pulling a prank on Roger Waters. They know he doesn't like walls, so they are blocking him from exiting his room which can be seen as a grid.

Roger Waters has a square grid of size `n × n` and he wants to traverse his grid from the upper left corner `(1,1)` to the lower right corner `(n,n)`. Waters can move from a square to any other square adjacent by a side, as long as he is still in the grid. Every cell except `(1,1)` and `(n,n)` has a value `0` or `1`.

Before starting his traversal he will pick either a `0` or a `1` and will be able to only go to cells whose values are equal to the digit he chose. The cells `(1,1)` and `(n,n)` are exempt from this rule. Because of this, cell `(1,1)` takes value `S` and cell `(n,n)` takes value `F`.

The rest of the band wants Waters to **not** be able to do his traversal, so they will invert at most two cells in the grid (from `0` to `1` or vice versa). They cannot invert cells `(1,1)` and `(n,n)`.

We can show that there always exists a solution for the given constraints. Waters will pick his digit **after** the band has changed the grid, so he must not be able to reach `(n,n)` no matter what digit he picks.

## Input

Each input contains multiple test cases. The first line contains the number of test cases `t` (1 ≤ t ≤ 50).

For each test case:
- The first line contains one integer `n` (3 ≤ n ≤ 200).
- The following `n` lines contain the binary grid, with `(1,1)` marked as `S` and `(n,n)` marked as `F`.

The sum of values of `n` doesn't exceed 200.

## Output

For each test case, output on the first line an integer `c` (0 ≤ c ≤ 2) — the number of inverted cells.

In the following `c` lines, print the coordinates of each inverted cell. You may not invert the same cell twice.

## Examples

**Input:**
```
3
4
S010
0001
1000
111F
3
S10
101
01F
5
S0101
00000
01111
11111
0001F
```

**Output:**
```
1
3 4
2
1 2
2 1
0
```

## Note

For the first test case, after inverting the cell, we get the following grid:

```
S010
0001
1001
111F
```
